point_001: The benchmark is artificially narrowed to only the first paragraph of Wikipedia articles rather than full articles, which sidesteps the core challenge of synthesizing information across long documents and limits the benchmark's utility for future work.
point_002: Reference passage selection uses simple word overlap or BM25 heuristics, which is a weak approach that could be improved by using entailment models, dense retrievers, or the same SPLADE model proposed elsewhere in the paper.
point_003: The filtering process for selecting reference passages uses term overlap with the target Wikipedia article, potentially leaking test-time target information into the retrieval corpus and giving an unfair advantage to retrieval-based systems.
point_004: The GPT-3 baseline uses a minimal prompt ('Introduce [Page Title]') that does not specify Wikipedia-style output, making the comparison unfair since GPT-3 is capable of generating Wikipedia-style text when prompted appropriately.
point_005: Automatic evaluation relies solely on n-gram overlap metrics; newer semantic similarity metrics such as BERTScore and BARTScore are absent, limiting the depth of the evaluation.
point_006: Many design choices (data filtering heuristics, retrieval filtering, warmup pre-training strategy) are not independently ablated, making it unclear which components actually drive performance improvements.
point_007: The modeling contributions in ReGen are largely incremental combinations of existing methods (SPLADE retrieval + FiD generation), offering limited technical novelty.
point_008: The query formulation is unnatural: queries like 'how google translate work' have a specific question-answering intent, whereas the supervision target (a Wikipedia lead section) provides broad encyclopedic information, creating a mismatch between query intent and generation target.
point_009: The dataset is English-only; a multilingual version would substantially broaden its usefulness to the research community.
point_010: The paper lacks qualitative analysis of model outputs beyond a small case study in the appendix, making it harder to understand failure modes and the nature of generated content.
point_011: No code is released alongside the paper, which hampers reproducibility despite the detailed description of implementation details in the appendix.
point_012: The steps for constructing the WebBrain-R and WebBrain-G dataset splits are described unclearly, making it difficult to fully understand or replicate the data pipeline.
point_013: The paper does not adequately address copyright and privacy concerns associated with releasing multiple terabytes of crawled web data that may contain copyrighted or personally identifiable content.
